---
title: "Command line"
---

This document describes the command-line interface (CLI) with
signatures, documentation, some pseudocode, and diagrams that represent
its interface along with explanations for these design decisions. It
primarily serves as a reference for us to develop from and track what
has been finished and what remains to be done.

{{< include /docs/includes/_design-status.qmd >}}

## {{< var planned >}} `check-datapackage`

`check-datapackage` has the following signature:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage <COMMAND> [PARAMETERS]
```

When used on its own it will show the help message with available
commands:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage
```

The default behavior shows the help message to prevent accidental
execution of commands without the user intending to invoke them. Plus,
it helps as a quick way of listing what the available commands are
without having to use `--help`.

If the command is used regularly, it might be useful to set up an alias
in the Terminal for it, e.g.:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
alias cdp="check-datapackage"
```

We intentionally use the same name as the Python package and repository,
`check-datapackage`, to give a consistent experience when installing and
using the package. Some other projects use different names for the CLI,
e.g. `cz` for the `commitizen` package, but we believe that using the
same name reduces confusion.

There will be two commands available:

- `check`: Check a Data Package's properties against the Data Package
  standard.

- `explain`: Explain issues found when checking a Data Package's
  properties in a human-readable format.

## {{< var planned >}} `check`

The `check` command has the following signature, that is positional as
well as keyword-based:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check [SOURCE] [CONFIG] [ERROR] [OUTPUT] [VERBOSE]
```

And when used, it would look like this:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check SOURCE --config CONFIG --error --output OUTPUT
```

The diagram below shows a simple flow for how the inputs and outputs
work for `check`.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-cli-check-flow
%%| fig-cap: "The flow of input and output through the CLI `check` command."
%%| fig-alt: "Diagram showing the flow of input and output through the CLI `check` command. The user provides the source of the `datapackage.json` file and optional configuration, which are used by the `check` command to output issues."
flowchart LR
    source("datapackage.json<br>[SOURCE: file, https,<br>gh/github]")
    config_file[".cdp.toml or<br>pyproject.toml<br>[file]")
    config_opt("--config<br>[option]")
    error_opt("--error<br>[flag]")
    output_opt("--output<br>[option]")
    verbose_opt("--verbose<br>[option]")
    check("check")
    file{"file"}
    cli{"cli"}
    issues["Issues<br>[list]")
    stdout["Output<br>[Terminal]"]

    source --> check
    config_file --> file
    config_opt --> cli
    error_opt --> check
    output_opt --> cli
    verbose_opt --> check
    file -- "or" --> check
    cli --> check
    check --> issues
    issues --> stdout
```

Configuration can be provided either as command-line arguments (as shown
in the signature) or through a configuration file. The CLI command will
first look for a configuration file named `.cdp.toml` (or in
`pyproject.toml` under the `[tool.check-datapackage]` section) in the
current directory.

The main argument to `check` is the location of the Data Package file as
a `SOURCE` (e.g. a file path or a URL) to check against the standard.
`check-datapackage` supports the following sources:

- {{< var planned >}} `file://` or a path without `file://`
- {{< var planned >}} `https://`
- {{< var planned >}} `gh:` or `github:`

Depending on the source given, there are different behaviours:

- If nothing is given, `check` will by default assume that `source` is a
  file path to the current directory and look for a `datapackage.json`
  file there, so the source becomes `./datapackage.json`.
- If a relative path to a folder is given, `check` will parse it to the
  absolute path and append `datapackage.json`, so that the path becomes
  `/ABSOLUTE_PATH/datapackage.json`.
- `https://` sources must point to a raw file. We won't support using
  `http://` for security reasons.
- `gh:` or `github:` sources must be in the format `gh:OWNER/REPO` or
  `github:OWNER/REPO`. It's possible to add `@REF` to the end to specify
  a Git ref, which can be a branch name, tag, or commit hash. `check`
  will parse this source to the raw `https:` of the `datapackage.json`
  file in the repository, assuming the `datapackage.json` file is in the
  root of the repository. For example:
  - `gh:seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle` will parse to
    `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle/main/datapackage.json`.
  - `github:seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle@0.2.0` will parse to
    `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle/0.2.0/datapackage.json`.
  - `gh:seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle@main` will parse to
    `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle/main/datapackage.json`.

If you want to customise the checks, you need to create a `.cdp.toml`
configuration file in the current directory, put the configurations in
the `pyproject.toml` for Python projects under `[tool.check-datapackage]`,
or use the CLI options. The design of the configuration is described in
the [Configuration settings](config.qmd) document.

We don't allow customising the configuration via the CLI so that we can:

- Simplify the CLI implementation and avoid long commands. Especially
  considering that customising checks can get quite complex with
  multiple exclusions and extensions.
- Enforce reproducible checks by having the configuration in a file that
  can be version controlled, shared, and made visible to others, such as
  collaborators.

::: callout-note
When `check-datapackage` reads in the `datapackage.json` file, it checks
whether it complies with the Data Package specification using the
[`jsonschema`](https://python-jsonschema.readthedocs.io/) library and
will output an error if there are any issues found that aren't excluded.
:::

### Config file structure

The configuration file `.cdp.toml` (or `[tool.check-datapackage]` in
`pyproject.toml`) has this structure:

``` toml
# The Data Package standard version to check against.
version = "v2"

# Whether to check properties that must *and should* be included.
strict = false

# Exclude all issues related to the "resources" property.
[[exclusions]]
jsonpath = "$.resources"

# Exclude all issues related to the "format" type in the schema.
[[exclusions]]
type = "format"

# Exclude issues that are both a "pattern" type and found in
# the "path" property of the "contributors" field.
[[exclusions]]
jsonpath = "$.contributors[*].path"
type = "pattern"

# Require that the "description" property is included in the Data Package.
[[extensions.required_checks]]
jsonpath = "$.description"
message = "This Data Package needs to include a 'description' property."

# A custom check to ensure that all resource names are lowercase.
[[extensions.custom_checks]]
jsonpath = "$.resources[*].name"
type = "name-lowercase"
message = "The value in the 'name' property of the 'resources' must be lowercase."
check = "lambda name: name.islower()"
```

### CLI options

The `check` command supports the following CLI options that override the
configuration file settings:

- `--config`: Path to a configuration file. If not provided, looks for
  `.cdp.toml` or `pyproject.toml` in the current directory.
- `--error`: When present, treats any issues found as errors that stop
  program execution. When absent (the default), issues are returned as a
  list without stopping execution.
- `--output`: Output format for the results. Options are:
  - `human` (default): Human-readable output using `explain()`
  - `json`: Output issues as a JSON array
- `--verbose`: When present, outputs more detailed messages about the
  checking process.

We override settings in the configuration file if the user specifies an
option in the CLI. This will allow for quicker testing of different
configurations as well as preventing the unintended side effects that
might arise when a configuration is mixed with incompatible option
settings.

This overriding behaviour applies to the `--error` and `--output` options;
if they are used on the CLI, they override the corresponding settings in
the configuration file.

### Output format

The default output format is human-readable, which uses the `explain()`
function to convert the list of `Issue` objects into a formatted message.
For example:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check ./datapackage.json
```

Would output:

```
2 issues were found in your datapackage.json:

At resources[0].name:
|
| name: woolly-dormice-2015
|   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
The `name` property must match the pattern "^[a-z0-9._-]+$".

At resources[0].schema.fields[2].type:
|
| type: integer
|   ^^^^^^^^^^^
The `type` property must be one of the allowed types.
```

If no issues are found, the output is:

```
All checks passed! Your Data Package is valid.
```

If the `--output json` option is used, the output is a JSON array of
issues:

``` json
[
  {
    "jsonpath": "$.resources[0].name",
    "type": "pattern",
    "message": "The `name` property must match the pattern \"^[a-z0-9._-]+$\".",
    "instance": "woolly-dormice-2015"
  },
  {
    "jsonpath": "$.resources[0].schema.fields[2].type",
    "type": "enum",
    "message": "The `type` property must be one of the allowed types.",
    "instance": "integer"
  }
]
```

## {{< var planned >}} `explain`

The `explain` command has the following signature:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage explain [SOURCE] [OUTPUT] [VERBOSE]
```

And when used, it would look like this:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage explain SOURCE --output OUTPUT
```

The diagram below shows a simple flow for how the inputs and outputs
work for `explain`.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-cli-explain-flow
%%| fig-cap: "The flow of input and output through the CLI `explain` command."
%%| fig-alt: "Diagram showing the flow of input and output through the CLI `explain` command."
flowchart LR
    source("datapackage.json<br>[SOURCE: file, https,<br>gh/github]")
    explain("explain")
    output_opt("--output<br>[option]")
    stdout("Output<br>[Terminal]")

    source --> explain
    output_opt --> explain
    explain --> stdout
```

In many ways, `explain` works similarly to `check`, as it also takes a
source to the Data Package metadata. The key differences are:

- `explain` runs `check()` with `error=False` internally and then converts
  the issues to human-readable messages using the `explain()` Python
  function.
- No configuration file is used or looked for. The only way to change
  the output is to use the `--output` option with either `human` or
  `json`.
- The `--error` flag is not available for this command, as its purpose is
  to always return human-readable output.

The reason we provide a separate `explain` command is for users who want
to quickly check their Data Package without worrying about exit codes or
error handling. This is useful for quick validation during development or
for documentation purposes.

## Exit codes

The CLI will use the following exit codes:

- `0`: All checks passed (no issues found, or all issues excluded).
- `1`: Issues found (when `--error` flag is used).
- `2`: Error reading or parsing the `datapackage.json` file.
- `3`: Error reading or parsing the configuration file.
- `4`: Invalid command or arguments.

These exit codes allow the CLI to be used in scripts and CI/CD pipelines
where exit codes are used to determine success or failure.

## Error handling

When the `--error` flag is used, the CLI will raise a `DataPackageError`
exception that stops program execution and outputs the human-readable
explanation of the issues. This is useful for CI/CD pipelines where you
want the build to fail if the Data Package is invalid.

When the `--error` flag is not used (the default), the CLI will output
the issues in the specified format and exit with code `0` even if issues
are found. This allows users to inspect the issues and decide what to do
with them programmatically.

## Integration with Seedcase Flower

The `check-datapackage` CLI is designed to work well with
[Seedcase Flower](https://flower.seedcase-project.org), which uses
`check-datapackage` to verify that Data Package metadata is valid before
building documentation. When Flower calls `check-datapackage`, it uses
the Python interface directly with the `error=True` argument to ensure
that invalid Data Packages are caught early in the documentation build
process.

::: callout-note
Future integration with other Seedcase tools (e.g., Seedcase Sprout) is
planned, where `check-datapackage` will be used to ensure Data Package
validity before operations like data ingestion or metadata updates.
:::
